title: The Central Bank Balance-Sheet Trilemma
summary: Federal Reserve staff economists Burcu Duygan-Bump and R. Jay Kahn introduce a "balance sheet trilemma" framework showing that central banks can achieve only two of three goals simultaneously: a small balance sheet, low volatility of short-term rates, and limited market intervention. The note documents that the Fed's balance sheet expanded from ~$800 billion (6% of GDP) in December 2005 to ~$6.5 trillion (21% of GDP) in December 2025, and analyzes how reserve scarcity affects money-market rate sensitivity and volatility.
key_points: Federal Reserve balance sheet grew from ~$800 billion (6% of GDP) in December 2005 to ~$6.5 trillion (21% of GDP) in December 2025
Balance sheet reduction (QT) concluded on December 1, 2025; reserve management purchases began December 10, 2025
The trilemma posits central banks can achieve only two of three goals: small balance sheet, low short-term rate volatility, limited market intervention
When reserve buffers are thin, even small shocks cause outsized increases in short-term funding rates across markets
Repo market sensitivity to Treasury General Account (TGA) movements, Treasury issuance, quarter-ends, and foreign repo pool rises as reserves fall relative to outstanding Treasuries
Key liability drivers affecting reserves include TGA, ON RRP, and foreign repo pool, which mechanically alter reserve levels one-for-one
why_it_matters: This FEDS Note provides a structural framework for understanding the operational tradeoffs central banks face as they normalize balance sheets post-QT. Market participants monitoring money-market conditions, repo spreads, and Fed policy implementation tools need to understand that reserve scarcity amplifies rate volatility and may require more frequent central bank interventions to maintain rate control.
market_structure_context: The trilemma directly addresses market structure by explaining how reserve levels affect money-market plumbing. With fewer reserves, banks have smaller buffers against settlement obligations, causing them to demand higher compensation for parting with liquidity. This makes short-term funding rates (repo, Fed Funds) more sensitive to changes in liquidity supply (TGA swings, ON RRP usage) and demand (quarter-end effects, large payment flows). The Fed's standing facilities (SRP, ON RRP, Discount Window) serve as passive tools to manage reserve fluctuations, but cannot fully offset structural scarcity.

issues_found: Source body was truncated due to character limits; analysis covers introduction through figures on repo rate sensitivity and volatility. Remainder of document could not be verified, though the available content represents the core analytical framework and key findings.

notes: Source is a Federal Reserve economic research note (FEDS Notes) authored by staff economists. Publication date January 14, 2026 is recent relative to TODAY (2026-04-12). The document is substantive research on central bank operational framework and market structure implications of balance sheet normalization. Source body was truncated due to character limits; analysis covers the primary argument and key findings as presented.
